curator_notes: Sold to Benedikt Taschen; still owns
Sold to Luke Wood (Beats by Dre)
Sold to Jeremy Scott; James Bond film
Sold to Ron Burkle; record PS sale
First US home with attached garage facing street

full_address: 8 Sea Cove Dr, Rancho Palos Verdes, CA
100 Old Pascack Rd, Pearl River, NY
2371 Cove Ave, Silver Lake, LA, CA
1455 La Mesa St, Colorado Springs, CO

curator_notes: Sold January 2021 for record-breaking $2.15M in Maplewood. Featured in "The Marvelous Mrs. Maisel" Season 3.

full_address: 265 Glen Lake Dr NW, Atlanta, GA

curator_notes: Prairie School masterpiece. Art glass windows, integrated furnishings. Meticulously restored by Steelcase Inc. Free public tours.

full_address: 505 College Avenue SE Grand Rapids MI 49503
parsed_city: 505 College Avenue SE Grand Rapids MI 49503
curator_notes: T-shaped Prairie School. Brick and sandstone trim, ceramic tile, stained glass skylights. Marion Mahony Griffin design input. Available via Airbnb. NRHP listed.
Wright's first residential work in board-and-batten siding. Moved 9 feet east in 1966.
Board-and-batten construction. Part of White Lake cottage cluster.
Board-and-batten siding with rectilinear contours. Companion to Mary Gerts cottage.

home_name: Laura Robeson Gale Rental Cottages (Birch Brook)
curator_notes: Three rental cottages commissioned after Thomas Gale's death in 1907.
Part of exclusive Les Cheneaux Club. Wright-remodeled cottage in northern Michigan.
Two-story design with glass living room and red-brick fireplace. Overlooks Lake Michigan.
One of three Wright residences completed in Grand Beach 1916. Original blueprints at Art Institute of Chicago. Exterior significantly modified.
Three-level structure following natural topography. Butterfly roof. John Lautner's first supervision job. Only FLW home in Upper Peninsula.
Wright's 'favorite small house.' 1400 sq ft early Usonian for MSU art professors. FLW Building Conservancy purchased at foreclosure 2001. Public tours since 2024.
Concrete block construction with small glass panes. Part of planned Usonian community. One of four Wright homes in Okemos.
First Wright-designed house in Detroit metro. One of ~25 pre-WWII Usonians. Brick and cypress. Cantilevers over stream bed. Donated to Lawrence Tech 1978.
Wright's 'My Little Gem.' L-shaped Usonian. Original cost $3600. 1968 terrace by William Wesley Peters. Open May-November.
2700 sq ft hexagonal pattern, diamond grid. Named 'Snowflake.' Tom Monaghan owned 1983. Currently owned by daughter of Lewis H. Goddard.
Built for Lewis H. Goddard, friend of Wall family. Adjacent to Wall House. Previously owned by Tom Monaghan. Gated.
Only Wright building in Detroit city limits. Rare two-story Usonian Automatic. All concrete, 15-foot ceilings, 48-foot gallery. $1M restoration 2006-2010.
Only Wright home in Ann Arbor. 2000 sq ft triangular geometry—no right angles. Separate tea house by John H. Howe.
Wright design from 1938, built by Taliesin Associated Architects 1979. 1300 sq ft Usonian on 10 acres. One of last Wright designs constructed.

full_address: 10551 E Peterson Park Road Northport MI 49670
parsed_city: 10551 E Peterson Park Road Northport MI 49670
curator_notes: Built for weaver on five-foot square modules. Chicago common brick with seasoned ash and oak. Originally featured goat enclosure on roof. Very secluded. Views of Lake Michigan.
Diamond-patterned module plan. Stone and cypress. Built for inventor of Heathkit/Heath Company. Overlooks St. Joseph River from dramatic bluff.
Wright's last Michigan residential design. L-shaped fan design. Panoramic Lake Michigan views. Designed for pioneering female physician. 4 bed, 3 bath, 3154 sq ft.
Wright's last Michigan building commission. 3850 sq ft, 4-bedroom Usonian. Brick with mahogany trim. Cantilevered terrace over St. Joseph River.

significance: Part of The Acres Historic District (NRHP 2004). 1961 addition by John Howe and William Wesley Peters of Taliesin.
description: Usonian design with concrete textile blocks and Honduran mahogany. First completed home at The Acres. Features greenhouse, enclosed lap pool, and Wright-designed furniture protected by easement.
curator_notes: PRICE CUT from $2.25M to $1.444M (35.8% reduction Dec 2025). Agent: Victoria Krause Schutte, Christie's (708.214.8108). Preservation easement donated Dec 2024 to FLW Building Conservancy. Previous sale: $511K (2017).
Single-story Usonian I-style at The Acres. Part of community of Upjohn pharmaceutical scientists.

significance: Part of The Acres Historic District (NRHP 2004). Usonian Automatic with 3,000 hand-made textile blocks. Dorothy was WWII WASP pilot, Congressional Gold Medal recipient.
description: 3,000 hand-made concrete blocks, 10-foot glass facade, radiant floor heating, original FLW furniture. Award-winning 2016-2017 restoration. 5-star Airbnb with 440+ stays.
curator_notes: 2019 Visser Award, 2021 Wright Spirit Award for restoration. Travel + Leisure 'Michigan's Most Unique Airbnb'. $390/night. Agent: Fred Taber, Jaqua Realtors (269.806.6829). Previous sale: $368K (2016).
Only solar hemicycle design at The Acres. Custom concrete blocks. 2021 Wright Spirit Award. Available via Airbnb/VRBO.
First house in Parkwyn Village, Wright's 47-acre planned Usonian community. Textile block. Pinwheel plan. Recent restoration to original blueprints.
Hexagon-based design built in stages. Browns participated in construction—made 3000+ concrete blocks. Owned 1951-2002.
Listed at $790,000 in March 2024, sold for $670,000 in July 2024.
Last of four Parkwyn homes built. Only two-story Usonian in Parkwyn Village. $800000+ restoration invested. 3 bed, 2.5 bath, 2469 sq ft.

full_address: Cottage Row North Manitou Island MI
parsed_city: Cottage Row North Manitou Island MI
curator_notes: Attribution debated but supported by NPS. Roofline similar to Winslow House. National Park Service property. Part of Sleeping Bear Dunes National Lakeshore.
31000 sq ft limestone, 56 rooms, 1300-acre estate. Mahony designed original Prairie Style plans. Jens Jensen designed gardens.
Curved walls, sweeping cone-shaped roofs. Seats ~700. Deconsecrated 2017. DOCOMOMO US/Michigan preservation efforts. Threatened with demolition.

full_address: 3822 Red Arrow Highway Lincoln Township MI
parsed_city: 3822 Red Arrow Highway Lincoln Township MI
curator_notes: 57 rooms in six-pointed snowflake pattern. Cost $1 million to build. NRHP listed 1998, removed 2021 after demolition. Designed by Taliesin Associated Architects.
1600 sq ft single-story on narrow city lot. Stucco walls and unpainted gray unit blocks. Goodall was Wright's former draftsman who joined Dow's firm.
Only non-Wright-designed house at The Acres. Part of The Acres National Register district.
Dow's very first commission while at Taliesin. Prairie Style influence. Cost approximately $2500.
Named for Dow's mother. Modernist design serving Midland community.
Includes Alden B. Dow Museum of Science and Art. Major civic cultural center.
Dow's most significant religious structure. Octagonal design with seven banks of pews.
Occupies entire city block. Skylit chancel. One of Dow's largest religious commissions.
15000 sq ft on 89 acres along Chippewa River. Built for Dow's sister. First time on market after 85 years family ownership.
1600 sq ft for Dow Chemical plant engineer. A 1930s time capsule.
Unit block construction. One of Dow's early Midland residential designs.

full_address: 1411 West St. Andrews Road Midland MI 48640
parsed_city: 1411 West St. Andrews Road Midland MI 48640
curator_notes: Built for Dow's cousin Howard and Katherine Ball. Unit block construction. NRHP listed 1999.
Early educational design by Dow. NRHP listed.

home_name: Ann Arbor City Hall (Guy Larcom Building)
curator_notes: Renamed Guy C. Larcom Jr. City Hall in 1995. Modernist civic design.
Campus master plan and 12+ structures including Kresge Auditorium, Corson Auditorium, Interlochen Bowl.
Visitor center with 56-foot diameter Plexiglas dome (replaced 2024). Opened September 1964.
Prototype design for Leonard Refineries. Only surviving example of Dow's gas station designs. TODO: Research exact address.
Private school campus designed by Dow.
Listed at $775,000 in Jan 2023, now off-market. Last sale was March 2017 for $515,000.

significance: Wright's FINAL residential design (designed 1959, completed posthumously 1967 by John Rattenbury). One of only 14 circular Wright homes.
description: Circular 'Sun House' design with rose-tinted concrete blocks, Philippine mahogany interior, crescent-shaped pool with mother-of-pearl tiles.
curator_notes: Auction 2019: $1.67M. Fractional ownership available at $1.5M/share. On Airbnb/VRBO. 1994 renovation reduced 5BR to 3BR. Agent: Deanna Peters (480.250.5675). MLS #6807254.

significance: One of only ~7 Usonian Automatic houses ever built. Precast concrete block system with on-site molded blocks.
description: Usonian Automatic overlooking Arizona Biltmore LINKS Golf Course 18th hole. 438 windows, Cherokee red accents, 2 guest houses, 6-car garage on 1.67 acres.
curator_notes: Expanded from original 700 sqft. 2018 updates. Listed Dec 2025. MLS #6967677. Agent: Jan Kabbani, The Brokery (602.739.5050). Previous sale: $1.2M (2002).

client_owner: George Lewis II and Clifton Lewis
significance: Only private FLW residence in Florida. One of only 11 hemicycle houses Wright built. National Trust '11 Most Endangered' 2014.
description: Hemicycle design on 10 wooded acres. Ocala block and tidewater red cypress. Civil rights history: hosted interracial meetings 1950s-60s, faced bomb threats.
curator_notes: First time on market in 71 years. Listed Aug 2025. Agent: Brian Proctor, NAI TALCOR (850.599.5963). Spring House Institute est. 1996. Needs restoration.

significance: Early Prairie School. 1883 Victorian remodeled by Wright 1900-1906. Japanese influence from Wright's 1905 Kyoto trip.
description: 6,350 sqft Prairie masterpiece with 136 leaded glass windows, Roman brick fireplaces, oak trim. Restored after 1976 fire. Block from FLW Home & Studio.
curator_notes: SOLD Sep 2025 over list price, under contract within days. Previous owners: Mark & Sallie Smylie (2001-2025). Cook County PIN: 16071080320000.

home_name: Wichita State University Corbin Education Center

significance: Only completed Case Study House by Richard Neutra
description: Neutra's only completed Case Study House, built for dentist Stuart J. Bailey. Features ocean views and classic Neutra design with extensive glazing. Meticulously restored by Marmol Radziner.
curator_notes: Owned by late Sam Simon (co-developer of The Simpsons). Additions in 1950 and 1958.

description: Built for artist Elsa Sale and engineer Robert Sale, located high in the Brentwood hills. Features skylights throughout emphasizing original owner's artwork, with custom mosaics retained.
curator_notes: Recently renovated while retaining Neutra's original details.

description: Two overlapping stucco and redwood cubes angled into steep terrain. Split-level living space retains Neutra's original floor plan.

full_address: 301 Hughes Road, King of Prussia, PA
significance: Rare East Coast Neutra residence
description: One of only a handful of Neutra properties in Pennsylvania. Wooded hillside setting with seamless connection to nature.
curator_notes: One of the few East Coast Neutra residences.

full_address: 2300 Silver Lake Boulevard, Silver Lake, CA
significance: Neutra's own home and studio; National Historic Landmark
description: Neutra's own home and studio, built with no-interest loan from Dutch philanthropist Dr. C.H. Van Der Leeuw. Original destroyed by fire in 1963; rebuilt by Dion Neutra with father's oversight. Radical 'glass house' design with rooftop and balcony gardens.
curator_notes: Open Saturdays 11am-3pm. Tours by Cal Poly Pomona architecture students. Virtual tours and digital archive available online.

significance: Neutra's breakthrough work; first steel-frame house in US
description: Neutra's first major commission and breakthrough work. Built for physician and naturopath Philip Lovell. First steel-frame house in the United States with gunite (sprayed-on concrete), rooftop terrace, natural light optimization, and cross-ventilation.
curator_notes: Walk-up tours every Saturday, 12pm-4pm (hourly, last tour at 3pm). Featured in LA Confidential and Beginners. Purchased by art dealers Iwan and Manuela Wirth for $8.75 million (2021) for restoration.

significance: Built for Fallingwater patron; iconic mid-century desert architecture
description: Built for Pittsburgh department store tycoon Edgar J. Kaufmann Sr. (who also commissioned Fallingwater). 3,162 sq ft in cross/pinwheel shape on 2-acre estate. Features famous 'Gloriette' rooftop deck, sliding glass walls, pool and gardens.
curator_notes: Garden tours during Palm Springs Modernism Week (February); interior not accessible. Immortalized in Julius Shulman photographs and Slim Aarons' 'Poolside Gossip' (1970). Meticulously restored by Marmol Radziner in 1990s. Ranked among top 10 houses in LA by LA Times.

description: Recently purchased by German collector; being activated as artist residency with public programs. Second-story addition (1936) by Gregory Ain.
curator_notes: Restoration work beginning summer 2025. Will function as artist residency with public event program.

description: Premier short-term luxury rental located on Sunset Strip, positioned equidistant between Beverly Hills Hotel and Chateau Marmont.
curator_notes: Available as Airbnb alternative / luxury vacation rental.

description: Mid-century Neutra residence available for long-term rental.
Pinwheel plan on dramatic 16-acre site with exposed boulders and ancient oak trees. Features famous succulent garden by Ralph T. Stevens.
Dramatic Mulholland Drive location with classic Neutra design principles.
Bel Air residence with classic Neutra horizontal lines and glazing.

significance: AIA Award recipient
description: Pasadena residence recognized with AIA Award.

significance: Part of the Neutra Colony
description: Part of the 'Neutra Colony' - the richest and densest collection of Neutra houses in the world. Exemplifies indoor-outdoor integration.
curator_notes: One of 9 houses in the Silver Lake Neutra Colony built 1948-1962.

significance: Part of the Neutra Colony
description: Part of the Neutra Colony in Silver Lake. Custom designed for owner's happiness and well-being.
curator_notes: One of 9 houses in the Silver Lake Neutra Colony built 1948-1962.

full_address: 2440 N. Neutra Place, Silver Lake, CA
significance: Part of the Neutra Colony; Neutra family residence
description: Originally speculative home; Richard and Dione Neutra lived here 1963-1966. Son Dion lived there until his death in 2019.

significance: Won House Beautiful competition
description: Built for Ukrainian actress Anna Sten and husband. Won House Beautiful competition.
Los Feliz residence designated as LA Historic Cultural Monument.
Hollywood Hills residence meticulously restored.
Overlooks nature preserve. Flat roof on thin pillars appears to float above glazed rear section.

full_address: Sunset Plaza Drive, Hollywood Hills, CA
client_owner: Josephine Ain Chuey and Robert Chuey
description: Built for poet Josephine Ain Chuey and painter Robert Chuey. Features smooth stucco, horizontal lines, 'spider leg' supports, and floor-to-ceiling glazing.
curator_notes: On 2.5 acres. Previously listed for $6.25 million.

description: Boxcar-style mid-century modern built for choreographer Eugene Loring (Billy the Kid ballet, Funny Face film). Features oversized retractable glass doors, manicured lawn, pool, bamboo garden, and detached guesthouse by Steven Ehrlich.
Located in the Arroyo Seco valley. Designated Individual Historic Resource by Cultural Heritage Commission.

significance: Neutra's first desert project; AIA Award recipient
description: Neutra's first desert project designed as winter residence and exercise studio. 50+ letters exchanged between Miller and Neutra to perfectly customize design.
curator_notes: Original construction budget: $5,000. AIA Award recipient.

significance: Rare East Coast Neutra
description: One of only three Neutra homes in Connecticut.

significance: Notable residents included Charles and Ray Eames, Orson Welles
description: 8-unit building near UCLA; Neutra owned 4 units himself. Inspired by pueblo architecture with stacked dwellings and rooftop terraces.
curator_notes: Notable residents: Charles and Ray Eames, Orson Welles, John Entenza, Luise Rainer. LA Historic Cultural Monument (1988); National Register of Historic Places (2013).

significance: Part of Neutra's European villas series
description: One of 8 European villas Neutra designed 1960-1970. Features glass-walled living areas, reinforced-concrete walls trimmed with aluminum, minimalist style.
curator_notes: Neutra died in Wuppertal in 1970 while working on a nearby project.

significance: Part of Neutra's European villas series
description: Swiss villa designed during Neutra's final decade.

significance: Part of Neutra's European villas series
description: Swiss villa designed during Neutra's final decade.

significance: Part of Neutra's European villas series
description: German residence designed during Neutra's final decade.
Housing development near Rhine-Main airport, proving Neutra's success beyond luxury villas.
International residence in Venezuela.

significance: Part of Neutra's European villas series
description: Swiss villa designed during Neutra's final decade.

full_address: 1100 Lake Shore Road, Grosse Pointe Shores, MI 48236
significance: 60-room Cotswold-style estate on 87 acres; landscape by Jens Jensen
description: This 30,000 sq ft lakeside mansion was completed in 1929 for Edsel Ford, son of Henry Ford. The estate features English Cotswold architecture, original antiques and art collection, and grounds designed by Jens Jensen. Listed on National Register of Historic Places in 1979, designated National Historic Landmark in 2016.

full_address: 257 Ridge Road, Grosse Pointe Farms, MI 48236
significance: Tudor Revival masterpiece for music publisher; only 3 owners in nearly 100 years
description: This 1928 Tudor mansion was built for music publisher Jerome Remick. Features grand marble-floored foyer, 10-foot sculpted plaster ceilings, oak paneling with linen-fold carvings, and handcrafted leaded-glass windows including custom cherub stained glass. Situated on rare private acre.

significance: Half city block estate on 2.16 acres in coveted historic district
description: This exceptional 1915 property occupies half a city block on 2.16 lush acres in the Boston-Edison Historic District. Designed by Albert Kahn for Benjamin Siegel, it represents his residential work for Detroit's elite.

significance: LA Historic-Cultural Monument #860
description: Dramatic site-driven modernism set into a steep hillside with expansive views over San Fernando Valley. Features iconic butterfly roof with clerestory light, mahogany and Douglas fir walls, ceilings, and built-in furniture.
curator_notes: Underwent meticulous 5-year restoration (2017-2022) by Barbara Bestor and Jeff Fink. Four fireplaces, spa-like steam shower, swimming pool and jacuzzi.

significance: One of Schindler's most celebrated residential works
description: Known for innovative use of organic materials, spatial rhythm, and connection to nature. Renovated in 2025 by Escher GuneWardena and Terremoto Landscape.

full_address: 3624 Buena Park Drive, Studio City, CA 91604
description: Commissioned by a screenwriter and actor, sited above the street at the end of a cul-de-sac with sweeping cinematic views across San Fernando Valley. Features original Schindler built-ins and fireplace.
curator_notes: Cared for by only four owners across nearly eight decades, all creatives. Includes 400 sq ft writers studio/office later transformed by Barbara Bestor.

significance: Swimming pool rumored designed by Richard Neutra
description: Considered a late design from Schindler, radiates pure Schindler aesthetics. Features swimming pool rumored to have been designed by Richard Neutra.
curator_notes: Sold for $2,350,000 in January 2024. Back on market.

full_address: 835 North Kings Road, West Hollywood, CA
current_owner: MAK Center for Art and Architecture
significance: Considered birthplace of Southern California Modernism; possibly first modern house built anywhere
description: Schindler's own home, built of tilt-up concrete slabs and redwood panels. Rejected conventions of rooms and hallways for open, flexible spaces that flowed into outdoor courtyards. Conceived as experiment in communal living with four rooms (one for each adult), communal patios, shared kitchen, outdoor sleeping porches on roof.
curator_notes: Later inhabited by photographer Edward Weston and composer John Cage. Restored by Marmol/Radziner in 2003. Operated by MAK Center. Audio tours via Bloomberg Connects app.

full_address: 1242 West Ocean Front, Newport Beach, CA
significance: One of Schindler's greatest works; National Register of Historic Places (1974)
description: Internationally acclaimed masterwork. Living quarters suspended above two massive figure-eight-shaped concrete frames, protecting from sand and surf while creating visual spectacle.
curator_notes: Still owned by Lovell family descendants. Cannot tour interior; exterior viewing from boardwalk between Newport and Balboa Piers. Rare interior tours organized occasionally by MAK Center.

description: Originally four-unit (now five) apartment building showcasing typical Schindler characteristics: compact layout, exceptional natural light incorporation, built-in furniture, variable ceiling heights, private outdoor gardens.
curator_notes: Free and open during exhibitions, openings, and select program days via MAK Center.

significance: MAK Center Study Center
description: Originally designed as a model home, now serves as MAK Center's Study Center, housing international residency program, research library, and curatorial headquarters.
curator_notes: Open to visitors once per month. Free admission.

description: Restored 1938 R.M. Schindler home near Elysian Park trails featuring wide open western sunset views, deck with fire pit, and expansive windows.

significance: LA Conservancy Preservation Award (2010)
description: 16-unit complex designed for artist Herman Sachs on steep Silver Lake hillside. Like an ancient European hill town, Schindler stepped buildings up the hillside with garden walkways. An early example of 'plaster skin' design.
curator_notes: LA Historic-Cultural Landmark (2016). Meticulous restoration begun in Spring 2015 by current owner Paul Finegold with landscaping by Terremoto.

significance: LA Historic-Cultural Monument #895
description: Large for a Schindler at 2,400 sq ft, built with center-cut redwood and poured concrete on top of a ridge with views of San Fernando Valley. Richard Neutra designed the landscaping.
curator_notes: Featured on MAK Center Architecture Tour Spring 2025.

significance: LA Historic-Cultural Monument #506 (declared October 9, 1990)
description: One of Schindler's most beloved late works, built on steep hillside with street facade reminiscent of ship's prow. Unpredictable angles and vertical emphasis distinguish it.
curator_notes: Original owner Adolph Tischler wrote a love letter about the house for the LA Times.

full_address: 805 South Genesee Avenue, Los Angeles, CA
significance: LA Historic-Cultural Monument #122; considered among Schindler's best works
description: L-shaped house on flat site near LACMA with rear opening to garden via sliding glass walls. Represents Schindler's application of Space Architecture theories to create expansive spaces within modest footprint.

significance: LA Historic-Cultural Monument #690 (declared 2001)
description: Los Feliz residence restored by Marmol/Radziner in 2001 after purchase by Cameron Silver and Jeff Snyder in 1999.

Set on steep lot with views to ocean and mountains. Garage at street level, house above at 45-degree angle. Exemplifies Schindler's 'plaster skin' designs. Street facade presents modernist box with flat roof while gable roof is revealed on patio elevation.
Designed in downslope cascade to maximize hillside views. Row of eight piers elevates volume while creating porch underneath.
curator_notes: Restored by architect and contractor Jeff Fink in 1994-1995.

description: Triple-cantilevered facade culminating with dynamic tapered roof plane. Secret garden passageway links to neighboring Walker House.
curator_notes: Notable former owners: Ken Reiner (later commissioned Lautner's Silvertop), Bernard Zimmerman (SCI-Arc founder), Donald C. Hensman (USC professor).

significance: Schindler's final complete design, realized posthumously
description: Schindler's final complete design, realized after his death in 1953. Nestled on promontory overlooking Franklin Hills and Los Angeles, within view of Hollyhock House at Barnsdall Park.

full_address: Griffith Park Boulevard, Silver Lake, CA
significance: LA Conservancy Preservation Award (2010)
description: Radical stepped apartment building on steep hill creating five separate levels. Two mirrored buildings commissioned by Anastasia Bubeshko. Served as gathering place for artists; sculptor Gordon Newell created decorative elements.
curator_notes: Architect Gregory Ain was once a tenant. LA Historic-Cultural Monument (2006).

significance: San Diego Historic Landmark #117 (1977); Schindler's only San Diego work
description: Complex of 12 duplexes (Schindler's only San Diego work), later merged into 6 condominiums. Library of Congress notes significance as 'creative use of site, unique structural system, one of the early expressions of floor plan in which the garden penetrates the house.'
curator_notes: Some units 'passionately and completely renovated'; owners enjoy Mills Act tax savings. Only one unit relatively intact; two burned; rest altered.
